bids.reasoning: This is the opening bid at t=0, so I should start with my highest-utility offer. My most important issue is issueC (weight 0.5), where valueC gives 1.0 utility. IssueD (weight 0.2) prefers valueC (1.0). IssueA (weight 0.1) prefers valueD (1.0). IssueB (weight 0.1) prefers valueB (1.0). This gives me maximum utility across all issues.

It's very early (t=0.02), so I should stay close to my optimal bid. The opponent's offer gives me very low utility: issueA:valueA (0.20), issueB:valueA (0.20), issueC:valueB (0.38), issueD:valueA (0.23) = 0.1*0.20 + 0.1*0.20 + 0.5*0.38 + 0.2*0.23 = 0.02 + 0.02 + 0.19 + 0.046 = 0.276 — quite poor. Their emotional message is cheap talk contradicted by their lopsided bid. I'll repeat my near-optimal offer with a tiny concession on issueA (keeping valueD) — actually I'll maintain my opening bid since it's only t=0.02 and the opponent showed no real concession.

The opponent has made the same bid twice (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueA, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueA), which gives me very low utility (0.1*0.20 + 0.1*0.20 + 0.5*0.38 + 0.2*0.23 = 0.02+0.02+0.19+0.046 = 0.276). They're holding firm at their opening offer. It's very early (t=0.04), so I should stay near my optimal bid. My optimal bid scores ~1.0: issueA: valueD (1.0), issueB: valueB (1.0), issueC: valueC (1.0), issueD: valueC (1.0). I'll repeat my top bid since the opponent hasn't moved at all and I have no pressure to concede yet.

Their messages are emotional manipulation and don't reflect real concessions. We're only at t=0.08, very early game. I should maintain my high-utility bid but perhaps make a small concession on a low-weight issue to signal willingness to engage.
